content.full_text:
RAVI MEHTA
CHAIRMAN

FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION

March 12, 1997

Lynda Burgess, CMC/AAE
City Clerk
City of Diamond Bar
21660 E. Copley Drive, Suite 100
Diamond Bar, California 91765-4177

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. I-97-022

Dear Ms. Burgess:

This letter is in response to your request for advice on behalf of several candidates in the City of Diamond Bar regarding the Proposition 208 provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Since you are not requesting advice on behalf of a specific individual whose identity is provided, we are treating this as a request for informal assistance.²

You have inquired how to interpret sections 85301(e) and 85307(c) as they apply to the contributions from community property funds?

SECTION 85301

Section 85301(e) makes a distinction between contributions from a candidate and a spouse. This section sets forth an exception whereby contributions of a candidate's personal funds to his or her own campaign committee are not limited. A spouse is considered a "person" (Section 82047) under the Act and therefore is subject to the contribution limits for persons (Section 85301(a) through (d)), regardless of the source of the funds.

_________________________

¹ Government Code sections 81000 - 91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109 - 18995, of the California Code of Regulations.

² Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329, subd. (c)(3), (c)(4).) .

428 I STREET, SUITE 800, SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814 (916) 322-5660

File No. I-97-022
Page 2

A spouse may make a contribution to the candidate/spouse's campaign committee from
community property funds by following the procedure outlined in Regulation 18533 (copy
enclosed). If the spouse signs the check from the community property account, he or she is
considered the contributor for purposes of the contribution limits. The spouse may only
contribute as specified in section 85301(a) through (d).

If the candidate makes a contribution to his or her campaign account from the community
property account and signs the check, the candidate is considered the contributor and section
85301(e) applies.

SECTION 85307(c)

Section 85307(c) allows a candidate to contribute to his or her campaign committee in the
form of a loan which does not total more than $20,000 at any one point in time. The candidate's
contribution may come from community property funds. If the candidate signs the check from a
community property account to contribute money to his or her campaign account, the candidate
is considered the contributor (Regulation 18533).

If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at
(916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel

By: Jill Stecher
Staff Counsel, Legal Division

SGC:JS:ak

Enclosure

sections.question: You have inquired how to interpret sections 85301(e) and 85307(c) as they apply to contributions from community property funds?
sections.conclusion: A spouse may make a contribution to the candidate/spouse's campaign committee from community property funds by following Regulation 18533 procedures. If the spouse signs the check, the spouse is the contributor and is subject to contribution limits under sections 85301(a)-(d). If the candidate signs the check from the community property account, the candidate is the contributor and section 85301(e) applies (unlimited personal contributions). Similarly, under section 85307(c), a candidate may contribute to his or her campaign as a loan not exceeding $20,000 at any time from community property funds if the candidate signs the check.
sections.question_synthetic: How should sections 85301(e) and 85307(c) of the Political Reform Act be interpreted when candidates and spouses make contributions from community property funds?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC clarified that contributions from community property funds are attributed to whoever signs the check—if the spouse signs, contribution limits apply to the spouse; if the candidate signs, the candidate's unlimited contribution privilege applies. This distinction also applies to candidate loans under section 85307(c), which may come from community property if the candidate is the signatory.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Informal advice letter responding to request on behalf of multiple candidates. No formal QUESTION/CONCLUSION sections with headers, but question and conclusion are clearly present in the body. Document is marked as 'informal assistance' which does not provide immunity. Regulation 18533 referenced regarding procedures for community property contributions.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: You have inquired how to interpret sections 85301(e) and 85307(c) as they apply to contributions from community property funds?

CONCLUSION: A spouse may make a contribution to the candidate/spouse's campaign committee from community property funds by following Regulation 18533 procedures. If the spouse signs the check, the spouse is the contributor and is subject to contribution limits under sections 85301(a)-(d). If the candidate signs the check from the community property account, the candidate is the contributor and section 85301(e) applies (unlimited personal contributions). Similarly, under section 85307(c), a candidate may contribute to his or her campaign as a loan not exceeding $20,000 at any time from community property funds if the candidate signs the check.

embedding.summary: The FPPC provided informal advice to the City of Diamond Bar on how to interpret Political Reform Act provisions regarding contributions from community property funds by candidates and their spouses, clarifying that the contributor identity is determined by who signs the check.
